questiontext.text:
Consider these class declarations.

public class Person
{
    ...
}

public class Teacher extends Person
{
    ...
}

Which is a true statement?

I. Teacher inherits the constructors of Person.
II. Teacher can add new methods and private instance variables.
III. Teacher can override existing private methods of Person.
Which statement about subclass methods is false?

answer.text: Writing two subclass methods with the same name but different parameters is called method overriding.
A public method in a subclass that is not in its superclass is not accessible by the superclass.
A private method in a superclass is not inherited by its subclass.
Two different subclasses of the same superclass inherit the same methods of the superclass.
If Class1 is a superclass of Class2, and Class2 is a superclass of Class3, and Class2 has no overridden methods, Class3 inherits all the public methods of Class1.

questiontext.text:
Consider a program to manipulate digital images. The inheritance hierarchy is as follows:

DigitalPicture
    ↑
Picture
    ↑     ↑
Landscape    Portrait

You may assume that DigitalPicture and Picture have no-argument constructors, but that Landscape and Portrait do not have any constructors. Which of the following declarations will compile?

I. DigitalPicture p = new Portrait();
II. Landscape p = new Picture();
III. DigitalPicture p = new DigitalPicture();

Consider the following hierarchy of classes.

Bird
    ↑     ↑
Parrot    Owl
    ↑
Parakeet

A program is written to print data about various birds:

public class BirdStuff
{
    public static void printName(Bird b) { }
    public static void printBirdCall(Parrot p) { }

    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
       Bird bird1 = new Bird();
       Bird bird2 = new Parrot();
       Parrot parrot1 = new Parrot();
       Parrot parrot2 = new Parakeet();
    }
}

Assuming that all of the given classes have no-argument constructors, which of the following segments will cause an error?

Refer to the classes below.

public class ClassA
{
    public void method1() { }
    public void method2() { }
}

public class ClassB extends ClassA
{
    public void method1() { }
    public void method3() { }
}

The method1 method in ClassB is an example of

Refer to the classes below.

public class ClassA
{
    public void method1() { }
    public void method2() { }
}

public class ClassB extends ClassA
{
    public void method1() { }
    public void method3() { }
}

Consider the following declarations in a client class.

ClassA ob1 = new ClassA();
ClassA ob2 = new ClassB();
ClassB ob3 = new ClassB();

Which of the following method calls will cause an error?

I. ob1.method3();
II. ob2.method3();
III. ob3.method2();

Consider these class declarations.

public class Player
{
    public Player() { }
    public int getMove() { return 0; }
}

public class ExpertPlayer extends Player
{
    public int compareTo(ExpertPlayer expert) { return 0; }
}

Which code segment in a client program will cause an error?

I.
Player p1 = new ExpertPlayer();
int x1 = p1.getMove();

II.
ExpertPlayer c1 = new ExpertPlayer();
ExpertPlayer c2 = new ExpertPlayer();
if (c1.compareTo(c2) < 0)
    x = c1.getMove();
else
    x = c2.getMove();

III.
Player h1 = new ExpertPlayer();
Player h2 = new ExpertPlayer();
if (h1.compareTo(h2) < 0)
    x = h1.getMove();
else
    x = h2.getMove();

Consider the following class definitions.

public class Animal
{
    private String type;

    public Animal(String theType)
    { type = theType; }

    public String getType()
    { return type; }
}

public class Dog extends Animal
{
    public Dog(String theType)
    { super(theType); }
}

The following code segment appears in another class.

Animal d1 = new Animal("poodle");
Animal d2 = new Dog("schnauzer");
Dog d3 = new Dog("yorkie");

public static void display(Animal a)
{
    System.out.println("This dog is a " + a.getType());
}

Which method calls will compile without error?

I. display(d1);
II. display(d2);
III. display(d3);

Consider the following class definitions.

public class StrStuff1
{
    public void printSub(String str)
    {
       String s = str.substring(2);
       System.out.print(s);
    }
}

public class StrStuff2 extends StrStuff1
{
    public void printSub(String str)
    {
       String s = str.substring(1);
       super.printSub(s);
       System.out.print(s);
    }
}

StrStuff1 p = new StrStuff2();
p.printSub("crab");

What is printed as a result?

Consider the following class definitions.

public class Class1
{
    public void doSomething(int n)
    {
       n -= 4;
       System.out.print(n);
    }
}

public class Class2 extends Class1
{
    public void doSomething(int n)
    {
       super.doSomething(n + 3);
       n *= 2;
       System.out.print(n);
    }
}

Class1 c = new Class2();
c.doSomething(8);

What is printed as a result?

Consider the following declarations in a program to find the quantity baseexp.

double base = <a double value>;
double exp = <a double value>;

/* code to find power, which equals baseexp */

Which is a correct replacement for

/* code to find power, which equals baseexp */ ?

I.
double power;
Math m = new Math();
power = m.pow(base, exp);

II.
double power;
power = Math.pow(base, exp);

III.
int power;
power = Math.pow(base, exp);

Consider the squareRoot method defined below.

/** Returns a Double whose value is the square root of the value represented by d. */
public Double squareRoot(Double d)
{
    /* implementation code */
}

Which /* implementation code */ satisfies the postcondition?

I.
double x = d;
x = Math.sqrt(x);
return x;

II.
return new Double(Math.sqrt(d.doubleValue()));

III.
return Double(Math.sqrt(d.doubleValue()));

Here are some examples of negative numbers rounded to the nearest integer:

-3.5 → -4
-8.97 → -9
-5.0 → -5
-2.487 → -2
-0.2 → 0

Refer to the following declaration.

double d = -4.67;

Which of the following correctly rounds d to the nearest integer?

A program is to simulate plant life under harsh conditions. Plants die randomly according to some probability.

public class Plant
{
    private double probDeath;

    public void liveOrDie()
    {
       /* statement to generate random number */
       if (/* test to determine if plant dies */)
          ...
       else
          ...
    }
}

Which of the following are correct replacements?

A program simulates 50 slips of paper numbered 1 through 50 placed in a bowl for a raffle drawing.

Which of the following statements stores in winner a random integer from 1 to 50?

Consider the following code segment.

Integer i = new Integer(20);
int k = i.intValue();

/* missing code */

Which of the following replacements for /* missing code */ will set i to 25?

I. i = new Integer(k + 5);
II. i += 5;
III. i = Integer.valueOf(k + 5);

Consider the following code segment.

Integer x = new Integer(5);
Integer y = new Integer(5);

int z = x.intValue() + y.intValue();

Which of the following tests generates a compile-time error?

I. if (x == y)

II. if (x.equals(y))

III. if (x == z)

Consider the following code segment.

Integer x = new Integer(1342);
String s = x.toString();
System.out.println(s.substring(1, 2) + s.substring(2, 3));

What is printed as a result of executing the code segment?

answer.text: No output is produced because an exception is thrown.
No output is produced because a run-time error is thrown.

questiontext.text:
Consider the following code segment.

String s1 = "dog";
String s2 = new String("dog");
String s3 = s1;

Which of the following are true?

I. s1 == s2
II. s1.equals(s2)
III. s1 == s3

Consider the following code segment.

String s1 = "apple";
String s2 = "banana";

Which of the following expressions is true?
